diagrams.description: This timeline traces the development of Social Work from charitable relief to a global profession. Key milestones include the establishment of the Charity Organization Society (London, 1869) and Hull House (Jane Addams, 1889), marking the settlement movement; Mary Richmond’s ‘Social Diagnosis’ (1917) founding professional casework; the first schools of social work (Amsterdam 1899, Columbia 1898, Chicago 1920); the Great Depression and New Deal expansion of public welfare (1930s); the rise of psychoanalytic approaches (1940s‑50s) followed by the systems and ecological turns (1960s‑70s); the emergence of anti‑oppressive and empowerment practices (1980s‑90s); the introduction of evidence‑based practice (1990s‑2000s); and the contemporary focus on global social work, digital practice, and decolonisation (2010s‑2020s).
